author: Irenaeus of Lyon
interpretation: Irenaeus identified the beast from the sea as a future individual Antichrist who would concentrate all prior evil into a single tyrannical reign lasting three and a half literal years. He famously discussed the number 666, proposing several possible names (Lateinos, Euanthas, Teitan) while cautioning against premature certainty. The second beast (false prophet) would perform real miracles to deceive humanity into worshiping the first beast. Irenaeus insisted on a literal, future fulfillment.
context: Irenaeus's futurist reading — a single end-times Antichrist — became the dominant patristic position and remains foundational for premillennial interpretation. His caution about identifying the 666 name has been less frequently heeded.

author: Victorinus of Pettau
interpretation: Victorinus, the earliest known Latin commentator on Revelation, identified the beast with the Roman Empire and connected the 'mortal wound that was healed' to the Nero redivivus legend — the popular belief that Nero would return from death. He read the beast's persecution of the saints as reflecting both the Neronian persecution and a future intensified repetition under the Antichrist. The number 666 encodes Nero's name in Hebrew gematria.
context: Victorinus introduced the Nero redivivus interpretation of the beast's healed wound, an approach that modern preterist scholars like Beale have revived and refined with strong historical-linguistic support.

author: Joachim of Fiore
interpretation: Joachim interpreted the beast from the sea as Saladin and Islam, reflecting the crisis of the Crusades. He employed a historicist approach in which the seven heads of the beast represented seven great persecutors of the church across history, from Herod through the Islamic conquests. The beast from the earth (second beast) would be a false pope or pseudo-Christian leader who would arise in the final age. Joachim expected the Antichrist to appear imminently.
context: Joachim's historicist method — reading Revelation as a coded timeline of church history — became the dominant approach for the next four centuries and directly influenced Reformation-era interpretations that identified the beast with the papacy.

author: Martin Luther
source_title: Preface to the Revelation of St. John (1530 revision)
interpretation: Luther identified the beast from the sea with the papacy and the beast from the earth with the papal political apparatus, particularly the papal states and their claim to temporal authority. The 'mark of the beast' referred to submission to papal decrees and canon law. Luther saw the Reformation itself as the fulfillment of the angel's call in Revelation 14 to 'come out' of Babylon. This identification was not metaphorical for Luther — he genuinely believed the institution of the papacy embodied the Antichrist described in Revelation.
context: Luther's papal-Antichrist reading was shared by Calvin, Knox, and the major Reformed confessions. It drove Protestant identity for centuries and remains affirmed in some confessional traditions today.

author: R.H. Charles
source_title: A Critical and Exegetical Commentary on the Revelation of St. John (ICC)
interpretation: Charles applied rigorous historical-critical method, arguing that the beast from the sea is the Roman Empire personified in its emperors, with the wounded head representing Nero and the healed wound reflecting the Nero redivivus myth. The beast from the earth represents the provincial cult of emperor worship in Asia Minor, which enforced participation through economic sanctions (the mark). The number 666 is the Hebrew gematria of 'Neron Caesar.' John was addressing the immediate political crisis of emperor worship under Domitian.
context: Charles's commentary established the historical-critical consensus that Revelation 13 addresses first-century Roman imperial persecution, a reading now supported by most mainstream New Testament scholarship regardless of theological tradition.

author: G.K. Beale
interpretation: Beale advocates an idealist reading in which the beast represents every anti-God political system that demands ultimate allegiance, with first-century Rome as the initial and paradigmatic instance. The 'mark' symbolizes ideological and economic co-option by the state — not a literal physical mark — and functions as the Satanic counterfeit of the seal of God on believers' foreheads. Beale argues that John intentionally draws on Daniel's four beasts, combining them into one composite figure to show that Rome embodies the fullness of beastly opposition to God's kingdom.
context: Beale's idealist-eclectic approach has gained wide academic influence by honoring the first-century context while preserving the passage's relevance for every era where the church confronts state idolatry.
